context: teaching us, God's grace teaches us some things, denying ungodliness and worldly lust. Notice what he says, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present world.
point_summary: God's grace not only brings salvation but actively teaches believers to deny ungodliness and live soberly, righteously, and godly—serving as a divine prescription for conduct in the present world.

context: and purify unto himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works. These things speak and exhort and rebuke with all authority, let no man despise them. context: being justified how? By his grace. We should we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life

context: Titus chapter number three in verse number five

point_summary: Salvation is the prerequisite for genuine service to God, and the book of Titus is introduced as a text addressing sound doctrine and good works among the redeemed.

context: for the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men
point_summary: Grace, rightly understood, teaches believers to deny ungodliness and live soberly, righteously, and godly—refuting any claim that being under grace permits moral license.

context: Verse 13, looking for that blessed hope and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Savior, Jesus Christ
point_summary: Anticipation of Christ's return — the "blessed hope" — is presented as the proper posture of the believer living between salvation and glory.

context: verse 14, who gave Himself for us that He might redeem us from all iniquity and purify unto Himself a peculiar people zealous of what? Of good works
point_summary: Christ's redemptive work purifies a people who are consequently zealous of good works—not works that save, but works that flow from those already redeemed.

context: He says in chapter number three, notice in verse number eight, the Bible says this

context: not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us
point_summary: Salvation rests entirely on God's mercy, not human righteousness, and this settled foundation is what then compels the redeemed to be zealous in maintaining good works.

context: by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost, which He shed on us abundantly through Jesus Christ, our Savior
point_summary: Salvation rests entirely on God's mercy—not human works of righteousness—and it is this mercy-wrought redemption that then produces a people zealous to maintain good works.

context: that being justified by His grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life
point_summary: Justification by grace alone—not works—makes believers heirs of eternal life, and that salvation then produces, rather than requires, a life zealous of good works.

context: This is a faithful saying and these things I will that now affirm constantly that they which have believed in God, those that have been saved, might be careful to maintain good works
point_summary: Salvation by mercy alone (Titus 3:5) establishes that believers maintain good works not to obtain or retain salvation, but as the fruit of having already been redeemed and justified by grace.

context: These things are good and profitable unto men
point_summary: Salvation by mercy alone (Titus 3:5) establishes that believers maintain good works not to obtain or retain salvation, but as the fruit of having already been redeemed and justified by grace.

point_summary: Believers are called not merely to possess good works but to actively maintain them—giving deliberate, sustained attention to fruitfulness—because salvation produces fruit, not the other way around.

point_summary: Sound doctrine, commanded in Titus 2:1, must be grounded in Bibliology—the study of Scripture—because God's self-revelation in his Word is the foundation of all theology.

context: the beginning portions of the first chapter that Titus had been given a specific responsibility to go into Crete and to establish there Those things that were wanting and to ordain elders in every city and the subject matter of sound Doctrine is repeated a number of times in the first chapter

context: the book of Titus and the importance of speaking those things which become sound doctrine
point_summary: Sound doctrine, as grounded in Titus, stands in contrast to the empty "doctrine of vanities" represented by polytheistic idolatry, underscoring the necessity of teaching truth rooted in the one true God.

context: the grace of God that bringeth salvation, it's appeared to all men

context: pursuing something that does not please Him and His appearing

context: I'm looking for that day of your appearing
point_summary: Faithfulness to Christ's call and the avoidance of worldly distraction are measured against the horizon of his appearing, which alone will determine what truly mattered.

context: Titus says it this way in Titus chapter number one

context: for there are many unruly and vain talkers and deceivers, especially they of the circumcision
point_summary: False teaching is not a rare exception but a widespread reality, as Paul's warning of "many" unruly deceivers in Titus 1:10 demands that believers remain alert rather than naïve.

context: Titus chapter one, he cannot lie
